title: Purchase Discounts (2/10, n/30)
event: Reyes Co. buys $8,000 of merchandise on account, terms 2/10, n/30 (perpetual system). Prepare the entry when Reyes pays the invoice.

lines.label: full invoice
lines.why: The full recorded obligation comes off the books.
lines.trap: Debit the FULL 8,000 — the payable was booked gross; the discount shows up on the credit side.

lines.label: 8,000 × 98%
lines.trap: 2% off the INVOICE, not off some other base — 8,000 × 0.02 = 160 saved.

lines.label: discount reduces cost
lines.why: Perpetual system: the discount REDUCES the inventory's cost.
lines.trap: Not a revenue account, not Purchase Discounts — under perpetual it lands in Inventory itself.

lines.label: full invoice
lines.trap: Missing the window doesn't change the payable — only the cash.

lines.label: no discount
lines.why: Full amount after day 10 — an expensive delay (2% for 20 days ≈ 36% annualized).
lines.trap: No penalty account, no extra expense — you simply lose the savings.

memorize.body: Read 2/10, n/30 as: 2% off if paid in 10 days, otherwise the net (full) amount is due in 30.
Under perpetual, discounts, returns, and freight-in all run THROUGH the Inventory account — cost is whatever it took to get goods ready to sell.

questions.prompt: Invoice $8,000, terms 2/10 n/30, paid on day 8. Cash paid = ?

distractors.feedback: Day 8 is inside the 10-day window — take the 2% discount: 8,000 × 0.98 = 7,840.
That's the discount itself. The CASH paid is the invoice net of it: 7,840.

build.scaffold: Debit A/P for the full invoice either way. Within the window, the cash credit shrinks and Inventory absorbs the difference.
